content:
# Acts Chapter 12 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 239-240 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_012_page_001.jpg, ACT_012_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now about that time, Herod the king laid hands upon some who belonged to the church to mistreat them. **2** And he killed James the brother of John with the sword. **3** And seeing that it was pleasing to the Jews, he proceeded to arrest Peter also. Now these were the days of Unleavened Bread. **4** And having seized him, he put him in prison, delivering him to four squads of four soldiers each to guard him, intending after the Passover to bring him out before the people.

**5** So Peter was being kept in the prison, but earnest prayer was being made to God on his behalf by the church.

**6** And when Herod was about to bring him out, on that very night Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, bound with two chains, and guards before the door were keeping watch over the prison. **7** And behold, an angel of the Lord stood by him, and a light shone in the cell. And striking Peter on the side, he raised him, saying, "Rise up quickly." And the chains fell off from his hands.

**8** And the angel said to him, "Gird yourself and bind on your sandals." And he did so. And he said to him, "Wrap your garment around you and follow me." **9** And going out, he followed him, and he did not know that what was being done through the angel was real, but thought he was seeing a vision. **10** And passing through the first guard and the second, they came to the iron gate leading into the city, which opened to them of its own accord. And going out, they went forward one street, and immediately the angel departed from him.

**11** And Peter, having come to himself, said, "Now I know truly that the Lord has sent forth his angel and rescued me from the hand of Herod and from all the expectation of the people of the Jews."

**12** And having considered this, he came to the house of Mary, the mother of John who was called Mark, where many were gathered together and were praying. **13** And when he knocked at the door of the gate, a servant girl named Rhoda came to answer. **14** And recognizing the voice of Peter, from her joy she did not open the gate, but ran in and reported that Peter was standing before the gate.

**15** But they said to her, "You are mad." But she kept insisting that it was so. And they said, "It is his angel."

**16** But Peter continued knocking, and when they opened, they saw him and were astonished. **17** And motioning to them with his hand to be silent, he related to them how the Lord had brought him out of the prison. And he said, "Report these things to James and to the brothers." And he departed and went to another place.

**18** Now when day came, there was no small disturbance among the soldiers as to what had become of Peter. **19** And when Herod had searched for him and did not find him, he examined the guards and commanded them to be led away. And going down from Judea to Caesarea, he stayed there.

**20** Now he was furiously angry with the people of Tyre and Sidon. But they came to him with one accord, and having persuaded Blastus, who was over the bedchamber of the king, they asked for peace, because their country was nourished by the king's country. **21** And on an appointed day, Herod, having clothed himself in royal apparel and having sat upon the judgment seat, delivered a public address to them. **22** And the people cried out, "The voice of a god and not of a man!" **23** And immediately an angel of the Lord struck him, because he did not give the glory to God. And becoming worm-eaten, he breathed his last.

**24** But the word of God grew and multiplied.

**25** And Barnabas and Saul returned to Jerusalem, having fulfilled their ministry, taking along with them also John who was called Mark.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 12 is one of the most artfully constructed chapters in the book of Acts. It presents a dramatic triptych: the persecution of the church by Herod Agrippa I (vv. 1-4), the miraculous deliverance of Peter from prison (vv. 5-19), and the divine judgment upon Herod (vv. 20-23), framed by the summary statement that God's word grew despite human opposition (v. 24). The chapter's literary architecture is built on deliberate contrasts -- an angel frees Peter by striking his side (v. 7), while an angel strikes Herod dead (v. 23); the church prays fervently for Peter's release but cannot believe it when it arrives (vv. 14-16); a king who commands sixteen soldiers cannot hold one prisoner, yet the same king is felled by worms.

**Manuscript condition:** Acts 12 begins in the fourth column of page 239 (folio 119v) and continues across all four columns of page 240 (folio 120r), concluding in the first column with the transition to Acts 13. The text is well-preserved and clearly legible throughout both pages. Standard nomina sacra are employed (KU for Lord, THW/THN for God). No significant lacunae or damage affect the readings.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show strong overall agreement on this chapter's content. The primary points of divergence concern (1) the critical textual variant in v. 25 (eis/ex/apo Ierousalem), (2) the rendering of the Hebraism prosetheto in v. 3, (3) the degree to which the euphemism in v. 19 should be made explicit, (4) the rendering of the graphic compound skolekobr0tos in v. 23, and (5) the vocabulary for Peter's rescue and Herod's demise.

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as the formal equivalence approach best serves both the dramatic narrative of Peter's prison escape and the judicial tone of Herod's death scene. EMET's literal precision is drawn upon for key textual details (especially the Hebraism in v. 3, the compound "worm-eaten" in v. 23, and the manuscript reading in v. 25). PNEUMA's dynamic clarity enriches the Rhoda scene (vv. 13-16) discussion but is generally set aside in the final text where precision is required.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 1 -- "laid hands upon" (epebalen tas cheiras):** EMET renders "put-forth the hands," preserving the Semitic idiom literally. LOGOS renders "laid hands upon." PNEUMA renders "moved to attack." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "laid hands upon" preserves the biblical idiom (a recognized English expression with deep roots in the KJV tradition) while being immediately intelligible. EMET's "put-forth the hands" is too wooden; PNEUMA's "moved to attack" abandons the idiom entirely.

2. **Verse 3 -- "he proceeded" (prosetheto):** All three translators render the Hebraism prosetheto (literally "he added") as "he proceeded" or its dynamic equivalent ("he went after"). This is a significant Septuagintal construction (Hebrew vayosef + infinitive) that Luke deliberately employs to give the narrative a biblical flavor. SOPHIA retains "he proceeded" (following EMET and LOGOS) -- the Hebraism is smoothed into natural English while preserving its sequential force. A note on this construction is warranted given its importance as evidence of Luke's Septuagintal style.

3. **Verse 4 -- "squads of four soldiers each" (tetradion):** EMET preserves the technical term "quaternions." LOGOS and PNEUMA render "squads of four soldiers each." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1): "squads of four soldiers each" communicates the military arrangement to modern readers. The total of sixteen soldiers for one prisoner underscores the irony of Peter's escape.

4. **Verse 5 -- "was being kept" vs. "was kept":** EMET renders with the periphrastic imperfect "was-being-kept" (en teroumenos); LOGOS renders "was kept"; PNEUMA renders "was kept." SOPHIA renders "was being kept" -- the periphrastic imperfect in the Greek emphasizes the continuous, ongoing nature of the imprisonment, which heightens the contrast with the church's continuous prayer. The imperfective aspect matters here narratively.

5. **Verse 7 -- "raised him" vs. "woke him":** EMET renders "he-raised him" (egeiren); LOGOS renders "he woke him"; PNEUMA renders "woke him." SOPHIA follows EMET: "he raised him." The verb egeiro means both "to raise" and "to wake," but its primary sense is "to raise up," and in Lukan usage it frequently carries resurrection overtones. In a chapter about divine deliverance from death-row imprisonment, the double resonance of "raised" is worth preserving.

6. **Verse 10 -- "of its own accord" (automate):** All three translators preserve this remarkable detail. SOPHIA concurs: "of its own accord" captures the Greek automate precisely. The iron gate -- the final barrier of Roman imprisonment -- simply yielded before God's messenger.

7. **Verse 14 -- The Rhoda scene:** All three translators capture the humor of Rhoda's reaction -- she recognizes Peter's voice, is so overjoyed that she fails to open the gate, and runs inside to report while Peter stands locked out. PNEUMA's note that this is "one of the most humanly delightful moments in Acts" is well taken. SOPHIA preserves the scene's natural comedy by following the structure of the Greek closely: the participial sequence (recognizing... not opening... running in... reporting) conveys the breathless, tumbling quality of the moment.

8. **Verse 15 -- "It is his angel" (ho angelos estin autou):** All three translators preserve this statement, which reflects the Jewish belief that each person had a guardian angel who could appear in their likeness. The irony is profound: the church is praying for Peter's release but finds it easier to believe in an angelic doppelganger than to accept that their prayers have been answered. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "It is his angel."

9. **Verse 19 -- "led away" (apachthēnai):** EMET renders "to-be-led-away" and notes the euphemism for execution. LOGOS renders "led away to execution," making the euphemism explicit. PNEUMA renders "ordered them executed." SOPHIA follows EMET's approach and renders "led away" without the explicit gloss "to execution." The Greek apachthēnai is a well-known euphemism for execution under Roman military law, and SOPHIA preserves the euphemistic register of the original text. The meaning is clear from context; making it explicit flattens the language.

10. **Verse 20 -- "furiously angry" (thumomachon):** EMET renders "furiously-angry"; LOGOS renders "deeply angry"; PNEUMA renders "in a furious dispute." The Greek thumomachon is a compound of thumos (wrath/fury) and machomai (to fight), indicating a hostile, combative disposition rather than mere displeasure. SOPHIA follows EMET: "furiously angry" captures the compound force. LOGOS understates the intensity; PNEUMA's "dispute" shifts toward diplomatic language that obscures the raw hostility.

11. **Verse 23 -- "becoming worm-eaten" (genomenos skolekobr0tos):** EMET renders "having-become worm-eaten"; LOGOS renders "being eaten by worms"; PNEUMA renders "He was eaten by worms." The Greek skolekobr0tos is a single compound adjective (skolex + bibrosko), used only here in the New Testament. SOPHIA renders "becoming worm-eaten" -- preserving the participial construction and the compound adjective as a single English compound, which is both more faithful to the Greek morphology and more viscerally effective than the periphrastic "eaten by worms." The parallel with Josephus's account of Herod Agrippa I's death (Antiquities 19.8.2) confirms the historical event.

12. **Verse 25 -- The critical textual variant (eis/ex Ierousalem):** This is the most significant textual issue in the chapter. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion. EMET reads "out-of Jerusalem" (ex Ierousalem) and claims Sinaiticus reads EΞ. LOGOS reads "from Jerusalem" but notes Sinaiticus reads EIΣ ("to"), calling it a scribal error. PNEUMA likewise notes Sinaiticus reads EIΣ but translates "from" based on context. Upon examination of the manuscript image, the Sinaiticus reading appears to be EIΣ IΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ ("to Jerusalem"). SOPHIA preserves the Sinaiticus reading "to Jerusalem" in the text, consistent with our governing principle of manuscript fidelity. The contextual difficulty (Barnabas and Saul are clearly returning from Jerusalem per 11:29-30) is real, but our task is to render what the manuscript reads, not to emend it. The reader and the Council Notes provide the necessary context.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "put-forth the hands" vs. "laid hands upon" vs. "moved to attack" | "put-forth the hands" | "laid hands upon" | "moved to attack" | "laid hands upon" -- follows LOGOS; preserves biblical idiom in natural English |
| 1 | "assembly" vs. "church" | "the assembly" | "the church" | "the church" | "the church" -- 2-to-1; ekklesia rendered consistently as "church" in Acts narrative |
| 2 | "[with a] sword" vs. "with the sword" | "[with a] sword" | "with the sword" | "by the sword" | "with the sword" -- follows LOGOS; the article (te machaira) specifies the instrument of execution |
| 3 | Hebraism rendering (prosetheto) | "he-proceeded" | "he proceeded" | "he went after" | "he proceeded" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; preserves the Septuagintal construction |
| 3 | Parenthetical placement | Dashes: "-- and they-were the days..." | Separate sentence: "Now these were the days..." | Separate sentence: "This was during the Festival..." | Separate sentence: "Now these were the days..." -- follows LOGOS; cleaner English without losing the parenthetical nature |
| 4 | "quaternions" vs. "squads of four" | "four quaternions of-soldiers" | "four squads of four soldiers each" | "four squads of four soldiers each" | "four squads of four soldiers each" -- 2-to-1; communicates the military arrangement clearly |
| 4 | "bring-him-up to the people" vs. "bring him out before the people" | "to-bring-him-up to-the people" | "to bring him out to the people" | "to bring Peter out for a public trial" | "to bring him out before the people" -- follows LOGOS with slight modification; anagagein means "to bring up/forth"; PNEUMA's "public trial" is interpretive |
| 5 | Periphrastic imperfect (en teroumenos) | "was-being-kept" | "was kept" | "was kept" | "was being kept" -- follows EMET; preserves continuous aspect of the Greek periphrastic |
| 5 | "earnestly" (ektenōs) placement | "prayer was earnestly being-made" | "earnest prayer was being made" | "praying...urgently and without ceasing" | "earnest prayer was being made" -- follows LOGOS; adjective placement is natural English; PNEUMA's expansion adds "without ceasing" which is not in the Greek |
| 6 | "in-that night" vs. "on that very night" | "in-that night" | "on that very night" | "The night before" | "on that very night" -- follows LOGOS; captures the demonstrative emphasis of te nukti ekeine |
| 7 | "Rise up in quickness" vs. "Rise up quickly" vs. "Get up, quickly!" | "Rise up in quickness" | "Rise up quickly" | "Get up, quickly!" | "Rise up quickly" -- follows LOGOS; smooths EMET's literalism (en tachei) while preserving the urgency; PNEUMA's "Get up" loses the formal register of anasta |
| 7 | "he-raised him" vs. "he woke him" | "he-raised him" | "he woke him" | "woke him" | "he raised him" -- follows EMET; egeiro carries resurrection overtones in Lukan usage |
| 8 | "Gird-yourself" vs. "Gird yourself" vs. "Get dressed" | "Gird-yourself" | "Gird yourself" | "Get dressed" | "Gird yourself" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); zōsai is specifically about girding/belting; PNEUMA's "Get dressed" is too general |
| 8 | "garment" vs. "cloak" | "the garment of-you" | "your garment" | "your cloak" | "your garment" -- follows LOGOS; himation can mean garment or cloak; "garment" is more encompassing |
| 9 | "the-[thing] being-done through the angel is true" vs. "what was being done by the angel was real" | "the-[thing] being-done through the angel is true" | "what was being done by the angel was real" | "what was happening through the angel was real" | "what was being done through the angel was real" -- composite; takes LOGOS structure with EMET/PNEUMA's "through" (dia) rather than "by" |
| 10 | "the-[one] leading into the city" vs. "that leads into the city" vs. "leading out to the city" | "the-[one] leading into the city" | "that leads into the city" | "leading out to the city" | "leading into the city" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; the gate leads into the city from the prison complex |
| 11 | "sent-forth the angel of-him" vs. "has sent forth his angel" | "the Lord sent-forth the angel of-him" | "the Lord has sent forth his angel" | "the Lord sent his angel" | "the Lord has sent forth his angel" -- follows LOGOS; perfect tense "has sent" reflects Peter's present realization of completed action |
| 11 | "rescued me out-of" vs. "delivered me from" vs. "rescued me from" | "rescued me out-of [the] hand" | "delivered me from the hand" | "rescued me from Herod's clutches" | "rescued me from the hand of Herod" -- composite; "rescued" (EMET/PNEUMA) better renders exeilato than "delivered"; "from the hand" (LOGOS) preserves the idiom |
| 12 | "having-considered" vs. "having considered this" vs. "Once he realized this" | "having-considered" | "having considered this" | "Once he realized this" | "having considered this" -- follows LOGOS; synidōn means to perceive/consider; "this" supplies the implied object |
| 14 | "from the joy" vs. "from her joy" vs. "so overjoyed" | "from the joy" | "from her joy" | "so overjoyed" | "from her joy" -- follows LOGOS; the article with joy (apo tes charas) is made personal and natural |
| 14 | "she-did not open the gate" vs. "she forgot to open the gate" | "she-did not open the gate" | "she did not open the gate" | "she forgot to open the gate" | "she did not open the gate" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); the Greek says simply ouk enoixen ("did not open"); PNEUMA's "forgot" is interpretive |
| 15 | "You-are-mad" vs. "You're out of your mind" | "You-are-mad" | "You are mad" | "You're out of your mind" | "You are mad" -- follows LOGOS; mainē is rendered directly; PNEUMA's expansion is idiomatic but unnecessary |
| 15 | "It-is the angel of-him" vs. "It is his angel" vs. "It must be his angel" | "It-is the angel of-him" | "It is his angel" | "It must be his angel" | "It is his angel" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek is a simple declarative (ho angelos estin autou); PNEUMA's "must be" adds epistemic hedging not in the text |
| 17 | "having-gone-out he-went" vs. "he departed and went" vs. "he left and went" | "having-gone-out he-went" | "he departed and went" | "he left and went" | "he departed and went" -- follows LOGOS; exelthōn + eporeuthe rendered as coordinated verbs |
| 18 | "disturbance" vs. "uproar" | "no small disturbance" | "no small disturbance" | "no small uproar" | "no small disturbance" -- 2-to-1; tarachos means disturbance/commotion |
| 19 | **"to-be-led-away" vs. "led away to execution" vs. "ordered them executed"** | "to-be-led-away" (notes euphemism) | "led away to execution" | "ordered them executed" | **"led away"** -- follows EMET's approach; apachthēnai is a euphemism; SOPHIA preserves the euphemistic register; see Council Notes |
| 20 | "furiously-angry" vs. "deeply angry" vs. "in a furious dispute" | "furiously-angry" | "deeply angry" | "in a furious dispute" | "furiously angry" -- follows EMET; thumomachon is a compound of thumos (fury) + machomai (fight); captures full intensity |
| 20 | "the bed-chamber of the king" vs. "the king's bedchamber" vs. "the king's personal attendant" | "the bed-chamber of-the king" | "the king's bedchamber" | "the king's personal attendant" | "the bedchamber of the king" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; epi tou koitōnos tou basileōs describes the office; PNEUMA shifts from office to person |
| 20 | "the being-nourished of-them the country from the royal-[land]" vs. "their country was nourished by the king's country" | Literal participial construction | "their country was nourished by the king's country" | "their region depended on the king's territory for its food supply" | "their country was nourished by the king's country" -- follows LOGOS; clear rendering of the economic dependence; PNEUMA adds interpretive "food supply" |
| 21 | "royal garments" vs. "royal apparel" vs. "royal robes" | "royal garments" | "royal apparel" | "royal robes" | "royal apparel" -- follows LOGOS; esthēta basilikēn = royal clothing/apparel |
| 21 | "judgment-seat" vs. "judgment seat" vs. "platform" | "the judgment-seat" | "the judgment seat" | "the platform" | "the judgment seat" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); bēma is specifically a judicial/official seat; PNEUMA's "platform" loses the legal connotation |
| 22 | "were-crying-out" vs. "cried out" vs. "began shouting" | "were-crying-out" | "cried out" | "began shouting" | "cried out" -- follows LOGOS; the imperfect epephōnei can be rendered as simple past in English narrative |
| 22 | "[The] voice of-a-god" vs. "The voice of a god" vs. "This is the voice of a god" | "voice of-a-god and not of-a-man" | "The voice of a god and not of a man" | "This is the voice of a god, not a man" | "The voice of a god and not of a man" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "This is" which is not in the Greek |
| 23 | **"worm-eaten" vs. "eaten by worms"** | "having-become worm-eaten" | "being eaten by worms" | "He was eaten by worms" | **"becoming worm-eaten"** -- follows EMET's compound; skolekobr0tos is one word in Greek, preserved as one compound in English; see Council Notes |
| 23 | "he-expired" vs. "he breathed his last" vs. "and died" | "he-expired" | "he breathed his last" | "and died" | "he breathed his last" -- follows LOGOS; exepsyxen literally means "he breathed out (his soul)"; "breathed his last" captures the Greek precisely; PNEUMA's "died" is flat |
| 24 | "was-growing and was-being-multiplied" vs. "grew and multiplied" vs. "kept growing and spreading" | "was-growing and was-being-multiplied" | "grew and multiplied" | "kept growing and spreading" | "grew and multiplied" -- follows LOGOS; clean aorist summary; PNEUMA's "spreading" replaces eplēthyneto (multiplied) with a different concept |
| 25 | **eis vs. ex Ierousalem (critical variant)** | "out-of Jerusalem" (claims Sinaiticus reads EΞ) | "from Jerusalem" (notes Sinaiticus reads EIΣ, calls it scribal error) | "from Jerusalem" (notes Sinaiticus reads EIΣ, follows context) | **"to Jerusalem"** -- preserves Sinaiticus reading EIΣ; see Council Notes and Disagreement Log for full discussion |
| 25 | "having-fulfilled the ministry" vs. "having fulfilled their ministry" vs. "once they had completed their mission" | "having-fulfilled the ministry" | "having fulfilled their ministry" | "once they had completed their mission" | "having fulfilled their ministry" -- follows LOGOS; adds possessive "their" naturally; PNEUMA's "mission" is too modern for diakonia |
| 25 | "having-taken-along also John" vs. "taking along with them John" vs. "bringing with them John" | "having-taken-along also John" | "taking along with them John" | "bringing with them John" | "taking along with them also John" -- composite; preserves "also" (kai) from EMET and the "with them" (sym-) from LOGOS |

-- SOPHIA
